title: FollowBench: A Multi-level Fine-grained Constraints Following Benchmark for Large Language Models
authors: Yuxin Jiang, Yufei Wang, Xingshan Zeng, Wanjun Zhong, Liangyou Li, Fei Mi, Lifeng Shang, Xin Jiang, Qun Liu, Wei Wang (HKUST(GZ)/HKUST and Huawei Noah's Ark Lab)
venue: arXiv preprint (cs.CL), submitted 31 Oct 2023, v3 camera-ready 5 June 2024 for ACL 2024 main conference (per arXiv comments field)
claim: LLM instruction-following ability should be evaluated via fine-grained, objectively checkable constraints rather than holistic response-quality judgments, and difficulty should be systematically controlled by the number of constraints stacked onto an instruction. FollowBench operationalizes this with five constraint categories (Content, Situation, Style, Format, Example) and a 'Multi-level' mechanism that incrementally adds one constraint per level (1 to 5), evaluated with a hybrid rule-based/model-based (GPT-4 judge with a multi-level-aware prompt) protocol and three metrics (HSR, SSR, CSL); across 13 LLMs, all models degrade substantially as level increases and even GPT-4/GPT-3.5 consistently satisfy only ~3 constraints on average (Introduction; Abstract; Table 2).
novelty: The authors present two claimed contributions versus prior instruction-following evaluation work: (1) comprehensiveness across five distinct fine-grained constraint types (Content, Situation, Style, Format, Example) spanning >50 NLP tasks, versus prior benchmarks (Tang et al. 2023 on tabular format; Chen et al. 2022/COGNAC on knowledge-intensive semantic restriction) that each cover only one constraint type in isolation (§2.2); (2) a novel 'Multi-level mechanism' that incrementally adds one constraint per level (1→5) to the same base instruction, enabling precise localization of the difficulty level at which a model's instruction-following breaks down, rather than a single fixed-difficulty test (§1, §3). A secondary methodological contribution is the 'Multi-level-aware' LLM-judge prompt that shows the judge the full constraint-evolution path (not just the final instruction+response), which the ablation in §5.1 shows raises human-agreement from 79% (no evolution path) / 67% (Vicuna-style holistic quality prompt) to 88%.

protocol.description: Each of the 820 instructions (one per level, 1-5, per task instance) is presented to the model as a single, self-contained prompt containing the base task plus all constraints accumulated up to that level; the model produces one response per call with temperature=0, max generation length 2048 (§4.1). The paper explicitly states this is single-round: 'our current investigation is confined to single-round interactions, aiming to offer a controlled environment for evaluation' and flags multi-round evaluation as future work (Limitations section). For open-ended instructions, a separate, single-shot LLM-judge call (GPT-4-Preview-1106) evaluates the response using a 'Multi-level-aware prompt template' that additionally shows the judge the sequence of instructions across levels (the 'evolution process') so it can identify which constraint was newly added at each level, then outputs a per-constraint YES/NO verdict list (§3.2, Figure 4, Appendix C Tables 13-14 show the judge's literal output format: an enumerated list of the 'n added constraints' followed by a Yes/No rationale per constraint and then a final list like ['YES','YES','YES','YES','YES']).
protocol.observation_content: For the base generation call: the model sees the full instruction text at level n, which is the original task instruction plus n accumulated constraint clauses (all constraints stated up front, nothing hidden or staged incrementally to the generating model itself — the 'multi-level' structure is a difficulty ladder across separate instances, not staged feedback within one instance). Example instruction shown verbatim (Appendix C, Table 13, level-5 Content-constraint instance): 'Talk about domestic animal: == [3 example animal descriptions] == Could you list three more domestic animals that satisfy ALL of following conditions: 1. Do not talk about dog. 2. Their weights are less than 100kg. 3. Can swim. 4. Have 4 legs. 5. Are hairy.' For the judge call: the judge sees the instruction-evolution path (all levels' instructions) plus the model's response and is asked to enumerate the newly-added constraints for the target level and verdict each one (Figure 4 caption: 'Prompt template for model-based evaluation'; §3.2 prose: 'we illustrate the evolution process of the instruction and prompt LLMs to pinpoint the newly added constraint at each level'). No demonstrations/few-shot exemplars are used for the base generation calls in general (constraints are stated directly), except the dedicated 'Example Constraints' category, which is itself a 5-shot in-context-learning setup by design (Appendix A.3: 'we utilize the standard 5-shot prompting... 5 shots are equipped with 1 sampled question template and 1 sampled answer template', with n-1 noise/distractor templates injected as n increases).

representations.summary: The paper does not run a systematic input/representation-format ablation (e.g., JSON vs. prose, or varying serialization schemes) analogous to what some other papers in this corpus test. The closest representation-related manipulations reported are: (1) the LLM-judge prompt format ablation (§5.1, Table 3) comparing three judge prompt templates and their agreement with human raters; and (2) the decoding-temperature sweep (§5.4, Figure 6).
representations.judge_prompt_format_effect: Table 3: 'Ours' (multi-level-aware prompt showing the constraint-evolution path) achieves 88% agreement with human judges; 'Ours w/o ML' (same prompt but with the evolution process removed) drops to 79% (a 9-point decline attributed directly to removing the evolution-path context); the Vicuna-style single holistic quality-score prompt ('Vicuna-Single') achieves only 67% agreement. This is presented as evidence that exposing the instruction's evolution path to the judge materially improves constraint-verification precision (§5.1).
representations.decoding_temperature_effect: §5.4 / Figure 6: sweeping temperature τ (with τ=0 denoting greedy decoding) has a 'tangible influence' on instruction-following ability across the four tested models, with a 'sweet spot ... somewhere in the middle' between too-deterministic and too-diverse sampling; exact per-model numbers are only in the figure (not reproduced as a table in the extracted text), so precise deltas are unverified — missing from extraction (only the qualitative conclusion survives as prose).

models.name: GPT-4-Preview-1106
strategies: zero-shot instruction-following (direct prompting, no CoT/few-shot scaffolding described for base task)
also used as the model-based judge for open-ended instructions
models.key_results: Table 2: HSR L1=84.7, L2=75.6, L3=70.8, L4=73.9, L5=61.9, HSR Avg=73.4; SSR Avg=77.2; CSL=3.3 (highest of all 13 models). Table 4: AlpacaEval 97.7, MMLU 86.4, BBH 86.7. Table 5 (failure consistency): 42.2% (lowest/best of the 4 models tested).

models.name: GPT-3.5-Turbo-1106
models.key_results: Table 2: HSR Avg=66.2, SSR Avg=72.5, CSL=2.9 (second-highest CSL). Table 4: AlpacaEval 86.3, MMLU 70.0, BBH 70.1.

models.name: Qwen-Chat-72B/14B/7B
models.key_results: Table 2: CSL of 2.4 (72B), 1.9 (14B), 1.5 (7B); HSR Avg 55.3 / 47.3 / 40.6 respectively — largest open-source model in the suite but still well below GPT-3.5/GPT-4.

models.name: LLaMA2-Chat-70B/13B/7B
models.key_results: Table 2: CSL 2.1 (70B), 2.2 (13B), 1.9 (7B) — the paper notes 'the scaling law does not apply as effectively to LLaMA2-Chat-70B' since 70B underperforms 13B on CSL and on Format/Mixed categories despite outperforming on Situation constraints (§4.2).

models.name: WizardLM-13B-V1.2
models.key_results: Table 2: HSR Avg=52.5, CSL=2.2. Table 4: exceeds GPT-3.5 on AlpacaEval (89.2 vs 86.3) but trails badly on instruction-following CSL (2.2 vs 2.9), used by the authors as the key illustration that FollowBench captures a distinct capability from response-quality benchmarks (§5.2). Table 5 failure consistency: 57.3%.

models.name: Vicuna-13B/7B-V1.5
models.key_results: Table 2: CSL 2.1 (13B) / 1.7 (7B). Table 4: Vicuna-V1.5 exceeds LLaMA2-Chat on MMLU/BBH but 'struggles with instruction-following tasks' (§5.2). Table 5 failure consistency for 7B: 61.8%.

models.name: Baichuan2-Chat-7B / ChatGLM3-6B
models.key_results: Table 2: CSL 1.4 (Baichuan2-Chat-7B, lowest of all 13 models) and 1.6 (ChatGLM3-6B). Table 5 failure consistency for ChatGLM3-6B: 64.0% (highest/worst of the 4 models tested, i.e., once it fails a level it almost always keeps failing).

headline_numbers: All 13 models' HSR/SSR decline monotonically-ish from level 1 to level 5 (Table 2; §4.2: 'the performance typically diminishes as we progress from L1 to L5 for almost all models').

CSL (Consistent Satisfaction Levels) ceiling: GPT-4 = 3.3, GPT-3.5 = 2.9; open-source models cluster around CSL ≈ 1.4-2.4, i.e., 'the instruction-following upper bound for GPT-4 and GPT-3.5 is approximately 3 constraints ... open-source models typically have an upper limit of about 2 constraints' (§4.2, Table 2).

Format constraints show the steepest degradation and lowest floors of any category for several models: LLaMA2-Chat-70B and LLaMA2-Chat-13B/7B score 0.0-0.5 HSR across nearly all levels (Table 10), versus GPT-4's 87.5→42.5 HSR L1→L5 in the same category.

Example constraints (5-shot pattern-following with n-1 noise exemplars) are uniformly hard: even GPT-4's HSR Avg is only 50.7 (Table 11) versus GPT-4's 76-93 range in Content/Situation/Style (Tables 7-9), attributed to LLMs' limited robustness to noisy few-shot templates (§4.3).

Judge-prompt ablation (Table 3, §5.1): multi-level-aware judge prompt = 88% human agreement (exceeding the 85% human-human inter-annotator agreement cited in the same passage); without the evolution-path context = 79%; holistic Vicuna-style quality-score prompt = 67%.

Failure consistency (Table 5, §5.3): GPT-4 42.2%, WizardLM-13B-V1.2 57.3%, Vicuna-7B-V1.5 61.8%, ChatGLM3-6B 64.0% — stronger models are actually LESS likely to fail at all subsequent levels once they fail one, i.e., failures are less 'sticky' for better models.
failure_modes: Universal level-degradation: 'the performance of all tested models declines substantially with an increase in difficulty level (the number of constraints in an instruction)' (§1 Introduction, not Abstract — this is one of the paper's four 'principal observations' listed in the Introduction's closing paragraph).

Category-specific weaknesses: Situation and Example constraints are called out as 'more challenging for LLMs than others' (§1 Introduction, not Abstract — same principal-observations paragraph); Format constraints produce near-zero scores for several LLaMA2-Chat variants (Table 10) and the paper attributes general format struggles to models' difficulty with 'complex, structured outputs such as tables, JSON, HTML, or LaTeX' (§3.1 Format Constraints, citing Tang et al. 2023).

Noise-sensitivity in Example constraints: 'the observed performance decline is primarily due to the LLMs' limited training in processing such noisy inputs within context-based learning scenarios... not adequately prepare them to sift through and ignore irrelevant or misleading information' (§4.3).

Non-monotonic scaling: LLaMA2-Chat-70B underperforms LLaMA2-Chat-13B on CSL and on Format/Mixed categories despite outperforming on Situation constraints, i.e., 'the scaling law does not apply as effectively to LLaMA2-Chat-70B' (§4.2).

Decomposition between response quality and instruction-following: WizardLM-13B-V1.2 beats GPT-3.5 on AlpacaEval (89.2 vs 86.3) yet trails badly on CSL (2.2 vs 2.9); Vicuna-V1.5 beats LLaMA2-Chat on MMLU/BBH but underperforms on instruction-following (§5.2, Table 4) — shown as evidence instruction-following is a distinct capability axis from general quality/knowledge/reasoning.

Judge-prompt fragility: removing the constraint-evolution context from the LLM judge prompt drops agreement with humans by 9 points (88%→79%), and using a generic holistic-quality judge prompt drops agreement to 67%, i.e., naive LLM-as-judge protocols are much worse at objectively verifying discrete constraints (§5.1, Table 3).
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper explicitly restricts itself to single-round/single-forward-pass evaluation and does not test any interactive, multi-turn, or feedback-loop protocol for the models under test: 'our current investigation is confined to single-round interactions, aiming to offer a controlled environment for evaluation. Future research may extend its scope to multi-round conversations to comprehensively assess the instruction-following proficiency of LLMs in more dynamic and extended dialogues' (Limitations section, citing Kwan et al. 2024 as a pointer to that future direction). The 'Multi-level' mechanic is NOT an interactive protocol from the generating model's perspective — it is a difficulty ladder across separate, independently-sampled instances (level-1 instance, level-2 instance, etc., each a fresh single-shot call with all constraints for that level stated up front), not a turn-by-turn accumulation of constraints within one conversation. The only place the paper studies something resembling 'more demonstrations helping': (a) the Example-constraints category is inherently a 5-shot in-context task where difficulty is manipulated by increasing the number of distinct (potentially noisy) exemplar templates mixed into the same 5 shots (Appendix A.3), and this is the category models struggle with most, suggesting more/noisier demonstrations degrade rather than help; and (b) the ablation in §5.1 is about the JUDGE's prompt richness (showing it the evolution path), not the generating model's demonstrations, and shows richer context (the evolution path) substantially improves the judge's discriminative accuracy.

relation_to_candidate.position: FollowBench is single-shot on the generation side (each level-n instance is one independent forward pass containing all n constraints stated up front; explicitly NOT multi-turn/interactive per the paper's own Limitations statement) and mixed rule-based/model-based on the verification side. What's observed by the generating model per call: the full instruction text (base task + accumulated constraint clauses), nothing staged or hidden — all constraints are explicitly stated in the prompt (contrast with c22's 'discover, not copy' Selection constraints, where a conditional branch rule is deliberately NOT stated). Output shape: an open-ended natural-language response (a list of extracted entities, a short answer, a table, a story, etc.) — not a single fact/label, and for many task types (Content/Situation/Style/Format open-ended QA) it is closer to 'full response satisfying N side-constraints' rather than a few-word output. Verifier type: hybrid — rule-based exact-match/accuracy programs for closed-ended tasks (E2E, WIKIEVENTS, CONLL-2003, GSM8K, HumanEval pass@1, Text Editing/Summarization/MT with rule-based checkers per Table 1 and Appendix A) versus an LLM-judge (GPT-4 with a multi-level-aware evolution-path prompt) for open-ended tasks, producing a per-constraint YES/NO verdict list. Difficulty dial: number of stacked constraints, level 1 to 5, always of the SAME constraint category within a given level-ladder (except the dedicated Mixed category).
relation_to_candidate.overlap_with_c22: Both use a 'stack constraints onto a base task, difficulty = number of constraints' design and both log per-constraint pass/fail verdicts as diagnostic facts (FollowBench's judge outputs an explicit ['YES','YES','NO',...] list per Appendix C Tables 13-14; c22 logs 'per-checker verdicts... as diagnostic facts'). Both use an incremental-level mechanic as the difficulty dial (FollowBench levels 1-5 via one added constraint per level; c22's candidate entry explicitly credits FollowBench as the source of 'the level-ladder mechanic for its incremental difficulty dial'). Both frame the comparison of stronger vs. weaker models partly in terms of how many constraints can be reliably satisfied at once (FollowBench's CSL metric operationalizes exactly this; c22's strict all-pass 0/1 scoring over 3-5 atoms measures a closely related quantity).
relation_to_candidate.differences: (1) Verifier determinism: FollowBench's verification is only rule-based/deterministic for its closed-ended task subset (Content: Data-to-Text, Event/NER extraction; Mixed: Text Editing/Summarization/MT) — roughly half its instructions (per §3.2: 'nearly half of instructions in FollowBench are open-ended without reference answers') require an LLM-as-judge, which is explicitly the kind of non-deterministic, imperfect ('relies on prompt engineering, introducing an inherent imperfection' — Limitations) scoring c22's design deliberately avoids by using a fully deterministic checker library (exact word count, forbidden letter, keyword, casing, end-token) for every instance. (2) Constraint composition structure: FollowBench's constraints are additive/independent and always fully stated in the prompt (no conditional/hidden branching logic) — there is no analog to c22's Selection constraint ('if property P then rule A else rule B') that must be discovered rather than read off the prompt; FollowBench's 'discovery' burden, such as it is, falls on the JUDGE (identifying which constraint was newly added at a level), not on the generating model. (3) Output granularity: FollowBench instances mostly call for substantive multi-sentence or structured responses (lists, tables, extracted entities, stories) rather than c22's deliberate 'output is a few words' micro-generation. (4) Scoring model: FollowBench reports continuous-ish aggregate rates (HSR/SSR percentages, CSL as an average consecutive-level count across a group) rather than c22's strict per-instance binary all-or-nothing 0/1 score; FollowBench's HSR is the closest analog to c22's all-pass scoring but is reported as an average over many instances/levels rather than as the atomic per-instance label. (5) Protocol: FollowBench is confined to single-round evaluation by explicit design choice/limitation; c22 as described is also single-call, so on this axis they align, but FollowBench does not test or claim anything about model behavior over an interaction loop.
relation_to_candidate.what_c22_would_add: c22 would add: (a) a fully deterministic, unit-tested checker library reused verbatim from IFBench (in place of FollowBench's GPT-4-judge dependency for open-ended instructions, which the FollowBench authors themselves flag as an 'inherent imperfection' in the Limitations section), removing judge-agreement noise (FollowBench's own ablation shows judge agreement varies 67-88% depending on prompt design, Table 3) as a source of label uncertainty; (b) genuinely hidden/conditional constraints (Selection: 'discover, not copy') that the generating model must infer rather than being told outright, which FollowBench's constraint stacking does not include — all of FollowBench's per-level constraints are explicitly written into the instruction text (e.g., Table 13/14's five numbered conditions are all stated verbatim to the model); (c) strict micro-scale output (a few words) enabling exact, unambiguous per-atom checking without the extraction/parsing/NLI-style judgment FollowBench's rule-based checkers still require for tasks like NER or event-argument extraction; (d) synthetic infinite generation (contamination-proofing) versus FollowBench's fixed, released 820-instruction (or 790 for -zh) static dataset, which is now public on GitHub and thus exposed to potential contamination in models trained after its 2023-2024 release.

corpus_claim: FollowBench (Jiang et al., ACL 2024) provides multi-level incremental constraint stacking (levels 1-5); c22 borrows the level-ladder mechanic for its incremental difficulty dial.
corpus_claim_verdict: supported. The paper verbatim confirms a 'Multi-level mechanism that incrementally adds a single constraint to the initial instruction at each increased level' producing 'a set of instructions ranging from 1 to 5 constraints' where 'level n' denotes an instruction containing n constraints (§3, §1 Introduction, Abstract). This is precisely the level-ladder/incremental-difficulty mechanic the corpus claim describes, and the venue/authorship (Jiang et al., ACL 2024 camera-ready per arXiv v3 comments field) matches as cited. No correction needed.
baseline_relevant: Instance/dataset scale: 820 total instructions across 5 constraint categories x ~50 NLP tasks (Table 1), each expanded to 5 difficulty levels; FollowBench-zh adds 790 more Chinese instructions (Appendix D, Table 15). Both are publicly released on GitHub (https://github.com/YJiangcm/FollowBench per Abstract), so treat as a plausible contamination risk for any model trained after 2023-2024 if used verbatim as eval data.

Difficulty dial = number of stacked constraints (1 to 5), same mechanic c22 plans to use; FollowBench's own results show degradation is fairly smooth but category-dependent (near-total collapse to 0-10% HSR for some LLaMA2-Chat variants specifically on Format constraints by level 3+, Table 10) — useful evidence that constraint-category choice, not just count, can create floor effects.

Metrics: HSR (Hard Satisfaction Rate, all constraints of an instance must pass — the direct analog of c22's strict 0/1 all-pass scoring) and SSR (Soft Satisfaction Rate, per-constraint average, i.e., partial credit) reported side by side, plus CSL (average number of consecutive levels satisfied from level 1). Formulas given explicitly in §3.2 (Eqs 1-3).

Ceilings/floors observed: best model (GPT-4) CSL=3.3 out of a maximum possible 5, HSR Avg=73.4%; worst tested model (Baichuan2-Chat-7B) CSL=1.4, HSR Avg=40.2% (Table 2). Format-constraint category shows near-zero floors for several 7B/13B/70B LLaMA2-Chat models (0.0 HSR at multiple levels, Table 10) — relevant if c22 baseline includes format/casing-style checkers, since even fairly capable models can floor out on rigid formatting-style constraints.

Prompt format that swung accuracy: for the LLM-JUDGE (not the generating model), exposing the constraint-evolution path in the judge prompt raised human-agreement from 79% to 88%, versus 67% for a generic holistic quality-score judge prompt (Table 3, §5.1) — relevant only if c22 ever needs an LLM-judge fallback; for c22's fully deterministic checkers this concern does not directly apply, but confirms that ambiguous/underspecified verification prompts materially degrade measurement fidelity.

Decoding parameters used: temperature=0 (greedy) for the main experiments to ensure deterministic outputs, max generation length 2048 (§4.1); a separate temperature sweep (§5.4, Figure 6, numbers not captured in extraction) shows accuracy is sensitive to temperature with a claimed 'sweet spot' between 0 and high values.

Contamination/data-release status: dataset and code are 'publicly available at https://github.com/YJiangcm/FollowBench' (Abstract) since the 2023 arXiv v1 / 2024 ACL camera-ready — a static, released benchmark, unlike c22's proposed synthetic infinite-pool generation designed specifically to avoid this exposure.

Known extraction gap: Table 12 (Mixed Constraints detailed results across 5 levels) was not captured in the local extraction (marked as a genuine gap in the source file); category-level Mixed-constraint numbers beyond the Figure 5 HSR-by-category chart are therefore unverified from this extraction.
quotes: "We introduce a Multi-level mechanism that incrementally adds a single constraint to the initial instruction at each increased level." (Abstract)

"Rather than merely presenting the instruction and asking LLMs to determine whether all constraints are satisfied, we illustrate the evolution process of the instruction and prompt LLMs to pinpoint the newly added constraint at each level." (§3.2 Evaluation Protocol)

"Regarding CSL, it can be deduced that the instruction-following upper bound for GPT-4 and GPT-3.5 is approximately 3 constraints (level 3) added to an initial instruction. In contrast, open-source models typically have an upper limit of about 2 constraints (level 2)." (§4.2)

"Our prompt template (Figure 4) registers an impressive 88% agreement with expert human evaluations, surpassing even the internal agreement among human experts, which stands at 85%. Remarkably, when the evolution process of multi-level constraints is removed from our prompt template, the agreement rate dips by 9%." (§5.1)

"Firstly, our current investigation is confined to single-round interactions, aiming to offer a controlled environment for evaluation. Future research may extend its scope to multi-round conversations..." (Limitations)

"the observed performance decline is primarily due to the LLMs' limited training in processing such noisy inputs within context-based learning scenarios. Typically, LLMs are fine-tuned on clean and uniform datasets, which do not adequately prepare them to sift through and ignore irrelevant or misleading information." (§4.3, on Example constraints)

"For instructions at difficulty level n (1<n≤5), the 5 shots are randomly paired with n question templates and n corresponding answer templates. Based on the question template of the query, the model is required to recognize the matched question template in the 5 shots and respond using the corresponding answer template." (Appendix A.3, Example Constraints)

"HSR = (1/m) Σ Π s_i^j ... where s_i^j = 1 if the j-th constraint of i-th instruction is satisfied and s_i^j = 0 otherwise. HSR measures the average rate at which all constraints of individual instructions are fully satisfied." (§3.2, Eq. 1)

corrections: failure_modes[0] ('Universal level-degradation' quote — 'the performance of all tested models declines substantially...') was cited as (Abstract) but the quoted sentence is actually from §1 Introduction's closing 'principal observations' paragraph, not the Abstract; the Abstract does not contain this sentence. Citation corrected to §1 Introduction.

failure_modes[1] ('Category-specific weaknesses' — Situation/Example 'more challenging for LLMs than others') was cited as (Abstract) but this quote is also from §1 Introduction's principal-observations paragraph, not the Abstract. Citation corrected to §1 Introduction.
notes_no_correction_needed: All Table 2 HSR/SSR/CSL values for all 13 models cross-checked cell-by-cell against the source table — exact match, no column misalignment found.

Table 3 (judge-prompt ablation: Ours=88%, Ours w/o ML=79%, Vicuna-Single=67%, human-human=85%), Table 4 (AlpacaEval/MMLU/BBH per model), Table 5 (failure consistency: GPT-4=42.2, WizardLM=57.3, Vicuna-7B=61.8, ChatGLM3-6B=64.0), and Tables 7-11 (per-category HSR/CSL spot checks, incl. Format-constraint near-zero floors for LLaMA2-Chat variants) all verified exact.
All 8 items in the quotes[] array verified verbatim against source (accounting for the paper's own ellipses/multi-sentence concatenation) — no fabricated or altered quotes found.

protocol.mode='single-shot' confirmed correct and complete: searched the full source text for any interactive/multi-turn/dialogue track and found none outside the single Limitations-section mention of multi-round evaluation as unaddressed future work (citing Kwan et al. 2024) — the paper genuinely has only one (single-shot) protocol track, so there is no second interactive track being incorrectly flattened.

observation_content claims (full instruction stated up front with no hidden/staged constraints; Example-constraints category is the sole 5-shot exception; judge sees the evolution path) verified against §3.1, §3.2, Appendix A.3, and the Table 13/14 case-study verbatim instruction/response/evaluation text.

Instance counts (820 total instructions, summing Table 1's #Data column exactly; over 50 NLP tasks; FollowBench-zh 790 instructions) verified against Abstract, §1, Data Quality Control, and Appendix D text.

Model list, novelty/§2.2 prior-work characterization (Tang et al. 2023 tabular/HTML/LaTeX; Chen et al. 2022/COGNAC knowledge-intensive semantic restriction), and all remaining §-number/Table-number citations spot-checked and found accurate.
